name: Nav
description: Nav, in Slavic mythology, represents the realm of the dead and the spirits of the departed. Often envisioned as a dark and mysterious figure, Nav is less a singular deity and more a collective term for the souls of those who have passed on. The word 'Nav' itself translates to 'corpse' or 'dead' in some Slavic languages, highlighting the deity's direct connection to mortality and the afterlife. While not typically worshipped in the same way as other Slavic gods, Nav was deeply respected and feared. Rituals and offerings were made to appease the spirits of the Nav, ensuring they would not return to haunt the living or cause misfortune. Beliefs surrounding Nav varied across different Slavic tribes, but the common thread was the understanding that the world of the dead existed alongside the world of the living, and that the spirits of the departed could influence the fortunes of the living. Some accounts portray Nav as malevolent spirits, capable of causing disease, drought, or other calamities. Others suggest they could also offer protection and guidance if properly honored. The Veles god is sometimes associated with Nav, connecting the world of the dead with livestock and wealth. Overall, Nav embodies the Slavic understanding of death as a transition to another realm, one that is both feared and revered.
subtitle: Realm of the Dead, Spirits of Ancestors

keyMyths.title: Appeasing the Nav
keyMyths.description: Offerings of food and drink were left at gravesites to appease the Nav, ensuring they would not bring misfortune to the living.

keyMyths.title: Nav and Veles
keyMyths.description: Veles, god of cattle and wealth, is sometimes associated with Nav, connecting the realm of the dead with prosperity and abundance.

keyMyths.title: Nav's Influence on the Living
keyMyths.description: The Nav were believed to have the power to influence the lives of the living, either through blessings or curses, depending on how they were treated.
